metadata.summary: Kubernetes deployment strategies (Recreate, Rolling Update, Blue-Green, Canary) with risk/cost/rollback tradeoffs. Choose strategy based on acceptable risk, resource cost, and rollback speed.

content.title: Kubernetes Deployment Strategies
content.summary: Kubernetes offers two built-in strategies (Recreate and Rolling Update) and three advanced strategies via Argo Rollouts (Blue-Green, Canary, A/B Testing). 80% of Kubernetes outages stem from deployment errors. The default Rolling Update is zero-downtime but has medium rollback speed; Blue-Green provides instant rollback at 2x resource cost; Canary is the most risk-averse — it shifts traffic incrementally while Prometheus/Datadog analysis gates each step.

why.p: Strategy choice directly maps to acceptable risk, resource cost, and rollback speed. Using Recreate in production causes downtime. Using only native Rolling Update for critical services misses the metric-driven auto-rollback that Argo Rollouts provides. Canary analysis with automated success/failure thresholds eliminates manual "watch dashboards during deploy" practice and catches regressions before they reach 100% of traffic.

list.item: Any production Kubernetes deployment that requires zero downtime (Rolling Update minimum).
Critical services needing instant rollback — use Blue-Green.
High-traffic systems where a bad deploy must not reach all users — use Canary.
GitOps workflows — Argo Rollouts integrates natively with Argo CD.
Applications that cannot run two versions simultaneously (DB schema breaking change) — use Recreate or migrate schema first.
Dev/staging environments where downtime is acceptable — Recreate is simpler.
Teams without Prometheus metrics or observability — Canary analysis gates require metrics; fall back to Rolling Update.

section.name: strategy-comparison
section.p: Strategy comparison table, mechanics of each strategy, Argo Rollouts vs native Kubernetes, and progressive delivery workflow.

list.item: Recreate: downtime yes, risk high, complexity low, resource cost low, rollback slow — development/migration only
Rolling Update: no downtime, risk medium, complexity low, resource medium, rollback medium — standard production default
Blue-Green: no downtime, risk low, complexity medium, resource high (2x), rollback instant — critical services needing full validation
Canary: no downtime, risk very low, complexity high, resource medium, rollback instant — high-traffic systems with observability

section.name: rolling-update-configuration
section.p: Rolling Update is the default Kubernetes deployment strategy. Configure maxUnavailable and maxSurge explicitly; never rely on defaults for production services.

rule.statement: Configure maxUnavailable and maxSurge explicitly; never rely on defaults for production services.
rule.rationale: Default values (25% each) may cause too-aggressive rollouts for services with tight SLAs. Conservative settings (maxUnavailable: 1, maxSurge: 2) ensure only one pod is unavailable at a time while allowing faster rollout.

example.title: Conservative Rolling Update Configuration
example.code: strategy: type: RollingUpdate rollingUpdate: maxUnavailable: 1 # conservative — only 1 pod unavailable at once maxSurge: 2 # 2 extra pods during rollout for speed revisionHistoryLimit: 5 # keep 5 revisions for rollback

section.name: blue-green-with-argo-rollouts
section.p: Blue-Green requires Argo Rollouts controller. Two services: activeService (production traffic) and previewService (test traffic). Promotion switches traffic instantly.

example.title: Blue-Green with Argo Rollouts
example.code: spec: strategy: blueGreen: activeService: myapp-active previewService: myapp-preview autoPromotionEnabled: false # require manual promotion for production scaleDownDelaySeconds: 30 # keep blue running 30s after switch for in-flight requests prePromotionAnalysis: # optional: run analysis before promoting templates: - templateName: success-rate

section.name: canary-with-metric-driven-promotion
section.p: Canary deployment shifts traffic incrementally while analysis gates each step. Always configure an AnalysisTemplate with concrete success thresholds before enabling automated canary promotion.

rule.statement: Always configure an AnalysisTemplate with concrete success thresholds before enabling automated canary promotion.
rule.rationale: Canary without analysis is just a slow rolling update. The value comes from metric-gated promotion: if error rate exceeds threshold during any step, the rollout automatically aborts and reverts to stable.

example.title: Canary Steps with Analysis
example.note: Canary steps: 5% for 5min → 25% for 10min → 50% for 15min → 100%. Analysis at each pause checks error rate and p99 latency.
example.code: spec: strategy: canary: steps: - setWeight: 5 - pause: {duration: 5m} - analysis: templates: [{templateName: success-rate}] - setWeight: 25 - pause: {duration: 10m} - setWeight: 50 - pause: {duration: 15m}

section.name: argo-rollouts-vs-native-kubernetes
section.p: Argo Rollouts extends Kubernetes with advanced deployment strategies and automated analysis capabilities.

list.item: Native K8s: Rolling Update built-in, Blue-Green and Canary require manual scripting, no automated analysis
Argo Rollouts: Blue-Green and Canary built-in, traffic management via Ingress or service mesh, automated Prometheus/Datadog analysis, metric-driven rollback, deep ArgoCD integration

section.name: tool-selection-guide
section.p: Choose a deployment strategy based on application characteristics and business requirements.

list.item: Simple apps, low traffic: Rolling Update with native K8s Deployment
Critical apps needing instant rollback: Blue-Green with Argo Rollouts
High-traffic, metric-driven: Canary with Argo Rollouts + Istio or NGINX
GitOps workflow: any strategy with Argo Rollouts + Argo CD

section.name: pre-deployment-checklist
section.p: Pre-deployment checklist ensures all prerequisites are met before initiating any deployment strategy.

checklist.check: Image tagged with specific version (NOT latest) and vulnerability-scanned
Resource requests and limits defined for every container
Liveness, readiness, and startup probes configured and tested
Service and Ingress configured; TLS certificates provisioned
HPA configured with appropriate metrics
Pod Disruption Budget defined to protect against simultaneous evictions
Anti-affinity rules set for HA (prefer spreading pods across nodes/zones)
Deployment strategy selected and configured
Rollback command prepared and documented

section.name: rolling-update-checklist
section.p: Rolling Update checklist for safe production rollouts using native Kubernetes.

checklist.check: Current deployment healthy: kubectl rollout status deployment/name
maxUnavailable set to 1 (conservative) or appropriate value
maxSurge set to 2-3 for moderate rollout speed
revisionHistoryLimit set to 5 for rollback options
Monitor pod status during rollout; watch for CrashLoopBackOff
Verify metrics (error rate, latency) within SLA after all pods updated

section.name: blue-green-checklist
section.p: Blue-Green checklist for deployments requiring instant rollback capability.

checklist.check: Sufficient resources for 2x replica count during deployment window
Preview service routing verified to green environment
Smoke tests and integration tests on preview passed before promotion
Rollback command prepared: kubectl argo rollouts undo name
Monitor error rates and latency for 5+ minutes after traffic switch
Old (blue) pods scaled down after scaleDownDelaySeconds

section.name: canary-checklist
section.p: Canary checklist for metric-driven progressive delivery deployments.

checklist.check: Baseline metrics captured before canary deployment
AnalysisTemplate configured with success-rate and latency thresholds
Traffic management configured: Istio VirtualService or NGINX annotations
Verify canary pods receiving correct traffic percentage
Confirm analysis metrics being collected and evaluated
Abort if needed: kubectl argo rollouts abort name

section.name: rollback-commands
section.p: Essential kubectl commands for rolling back deployments using both native Kubernetes and Argo Rollouts.

example.title: Rollback Commands Reference
example.code:
# Native Kubernetes rollback kubectl rollout history deployment/<name> kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name> kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name> --to-revision=<N>
# Argo Rollouts rollback kubectl argo rollouts abort <name> # abort current rollout, revert to stable kubectl argo rollouts undo <name> # undo to previous version kubectl argo rollouts promote <name> # manually promote canary to 100% kubectl argo rollouts retry <name> # retry aborted rollout after fix

section.name: production-readiness
section.p: Production readiness checklist ensures security, observability, and reliability for all deployments.

checklist.check: Pods running as non-root with read-only root filesystem
Capabilities dropped (--cap-drop ALL); only required caps added
Network policies restricting inter-service traffic to minimum required
Secrets encrypted at rest; no secrets in environment variables or ConfigMaps
Prometheus metrics exposed; structured logging implemented; distributed tracing enabled
Alerts configured for key metrics (error rate, latency, saturation)
Pod anti-affinity and topology spread constraints configured
Runbook for common failure modes documented and accessible
